was an Australian flagship [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"), founded in 1972 in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). It published and distributed many successful Australian artists and expanded internationally, until it was merged with [Festival Records](/wiki/Festival_Records "Festival Records") in 1998. Festival Mushroom Records was later acquired by [Warner Bros. Records](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records"), which operated the label from 2005 to 2010 before merging the label's catalogue with its own. Founder [Michael Gudinski](/wiki/Michael_Gudinski "Michael Gudinski") went on to become the leader of the [Mushroom Group](/wiki/Mushroom_Group "Mushroom Group"), the largest independent music and entertainment company in Australia, with divisions such as [Frontier Touring](/wiki/Frontier_Touring_Company "Frontier Touring Company").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian record label

For the parent company of Mushroom Records, see [Mushroom Group](/wiki/Mushroom_Group "Mushroom Group"). For the UK offshoot label, see [Mushroom Records UK](/wiki/Mushroom_Records_UK "Mushroom Records UK"). For the unrelated defunct Canadian company, see [Mushroom Records (Canada)](/wiki/Mushroom_Records_(Canada) "Mushroom Records (Canada)").

Record label

**Mushroom Records** was an Australian flagship [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"), founded in 1972 in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). It published and distributed many successful Australian artists and expanded internationally, until it was merged with [Festival Records](/wiki/Festival_Records "Festival Records") in 1998. Festival Mushroom Records was later acquired by [Warner Bros. Records](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records"), which operated the label from 2005 to 2010 before merging the label's catalogue with its own. Founder [Michael Gudinski](/wiki/Michael_Gudinski "Michael Gudinski") went on to become the leader of the [Mushroom Group](/wiki/Mushroom_Group "Mushroom Group"), the largest independent music and entertainment company in Australia, with divisions such as [Frontier Touring](/wiki/Frontier_Touring_Company "Frontier Touring Company").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mushroom_Records&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Mushroom Records was an Australian [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") formed by [Michael Gudinski](/wiki/Michael_Gudinski "Michael Gudinski") and Ray Evans in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") in 1972.

Its inaugural release was an ambitious triple [live album](/wiki/Live_album "Live album") of the 1973 [Sunbury Pop Festival](/wiki/Sunbury_Pop_Festival "Sunbury Pop Festival"), and over the first few years of its existence Mushroom signed a number of important Australian acts including [Madder Lake](/wiki/Madder_Lake_(band) "Madder Lake (band)"), [Ayers Rock](/wiki/Ayers_Rock_(band) "Ayers Rock (band)"), and [MacKenzie Theory](/wiki/MacKenzie_Theory "MacKenzie Theory"). In December 1974, Gudinski flew to the US to promote his recording artists, with Ayers Rock securing a recording contract, and an advance from [A&M Records](/wiki/A%26M_Records "A&M Records").[[3]](#cite_note-Eliezer_98-3) Ayers Rock were the first Mushroom artist to sign an international recording contract.[[3]](#cite_note-Eliezer_98-3) Mushroom struggled to survive for its first two years and reportedly came close to folding on several occasions. However, the label was dramatically pushed to the forefront of the Australian music scene in early 1975 following the massive success of [Skyhooks](/wiki/Skyhooks_(band) "Skyhooks (band)"), whose debut album, *[Living in the 70's](/wiki/Living_in_the_70%27s "Living in the 70's")*, became the biggest-selling Australian LP at that time.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Around the same time, Gudinski was convinced to sign expatriate [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") band [Split Enz](/wiki/Split_Enz "Split Enz"), who had recently relocated to Australia. Although they had only moderate success for the first few years, Split Enz scored huge success in 1980 with the release of their album *[True Colours](/wiki/True_Colours_(Split_Enz_album) "True Colours (Split Enz album)")* and the hit single "[I Got You](/wiki/I_Got_You_(Split_Enz_song) "I Got You (Split Enz song)")", which marked the emergence of [Neil Finn](/wiki/Neil_Finn "Neil Finn").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1978, Gudinski started Suicide, a subsidiary label to Mushroom, to release punk music. Unlike Mushroom, Suicide was distributed by [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records"). Its first release was a cover version of "[These Boots Are Made For Walking](/wiki/These_Boots_Are_Made_For_Walking "These Boots Are Made For Walking")" by [The Boys Next Door](/wiki/The_Birthday_Party_(band) "The Birthday Party (band)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

In 1981, Gudinski started another subsidiary, White Label Records, to release what might now be called post-punk music, with signings such as [Hunters & Collectors](/wiki/Hunters_%26_Collectors "Hunters & Collectors"), [Machinations](/wiki/Machinations_(band) "Machinations (band)"), [Painters and Dockers](/wiki/Painters_and_Dockers "Painters and Dockers"), [Kids in the Kitchen](/wiki/Kids_in_the_Kitchen "Kids in the Kitchen") and [The Stems](/wiki/The_Stems "The Stems").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the 1990s, remixer [Gavin Campbell](/wiki/Gavin_Campbell_(music_producer) "Gavin Campbell (music producer)")'s Razor Records became a subsidiary of Mushroom.[[8]](#cite_note-contact-8)

Mushroom entered the international scene[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] by setting up an International division in London headed by Gary Ashley (deceased 2017). The Australian label had some international success before then by signing [The Saints](/wiki/The_Saints_(Australian_band) "The Saints (Australian band)"), [Dannii Minogue](/wiki/Dannii_Minogue "Dannii Minogue"), [Kylie Minogue](/wiki/Kylie_Minogue "Kylie Minogue") and [Jason Donovan](/wiki/Jason_Donovan "Jason Donovan") in the eighties. However the last two were released internationally through [PWL](/wiki/PWL "PWL").

After Gudinski sold 49% of the company to [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)") in 1993, [Garbage](/wiki/Garbage_(band) "Garbage (band)") signed to the label via [Koda Marshall](/w/index.php?title=Koda_Marshall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Koda Marshall (page does not exist)")'s [UK operation](/wiki/A%26E_Records "A&E Records"), followed by [Pop Will Eat Itself](/wiki/Pop_Will_Eat_Itself "Pop Will Eat Itself"), [Ash](/wiki/Ash_(band) "Ash (band)"), [The Paradise Motel](/wiki/The_Paradise_Motel "The Paradise Motel") and [Peter André](/wiki/Peter_Andr%C3%A9 "Peter André").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Gudinski sold the remaining 51% share of the label to News Corporation in 1998 for a sum reported to have been around A$40 million. However, the sale of Australia's last major independent label to a multinational engendered some adverse publicity, with Gudinski controversially claiming that he had sold the label as a protest at the federal government's changes to the regulations governing the parallel importation of recordings.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Gudinski maintained control of most other Mushroom Group companies (including interests in [music publishing](/wiki/Music_publisher_(popular_music) "Music publisher (popular music)"), film production, band booking, national touring and venue management) and the name Mushroom Records. When Mushroom was merged with News Corporation's record label [Festival Records](/wiki/Festival_Records_(Australia) "Festival Records (Australia)") to form Festival Mushroom Records in 1998, Gudinski relaunched [Liberation Music](/wiki/Liberation_Music "Liberation Music").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In October 2005, Festival Mushroom Records was wound up and its trademarks and assets (including its large archive of master recordings) were sold to the Australian division of the [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") in a deal reported to be worth around A$10 million.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The company's other major asset, Festival Music Publishing, was sold to Gudinski's Mushroom Music a month later for an undisclosed sum.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In December 2009, Gudinski announced that he bought the remaining 50% he did not own from Warner Bros. In 2010, it became fully independent again.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2021, Gudinski died in Melbourne at the age of 68.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Throughout 2023, the company celebrated their fiftieth anniversary with a yearlong celebration titled, *[Mushroom: Fifty Years of Making Noise](/wiki/Mushroom:_Fifty_Years_of_Making_Noise "Mushroom: Fifty Years of Making Noise")*.

## A&E Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mushroom_Records&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: A&E Records")]

With the reorganisation into [Festival Mushroom Records](/wiki/Festival_Mushroom_Records "Festival Mushroom Records"), a number of international operations and joint ventures were either shut down or sold. In the United Kingdom, the British arm of the company (including [Infectious](/wiki/Infectious_Records "Infectious Records") and [Perfecto Records](/wiki/Perfecto_Records "Perfecto Records")) was reorganised as [A&E Records](/wiki/A%26E_Records "A&E Records") and sold on to the [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") when [Korda Marshall](/wiki/Korda_Marshall "Korda Marshall") took a position within that company.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
